Thunderstorms, hail to lash Chandigarh, Punjab, Haryana; IMD issues orange alert
IMD predicts partly cloudy skies with thunderstorms and light rain activity on April 3 and 4, followed by mainly partly cloudy conditions
A fresh spell of stormy weather is set to hit Chandigarh, Mohali, and Panchkula, with the India Meteorological Department (IMD) issuing an orange alert for Friday and Saturday, warning of thunderstorms, lightning, gusty winds reaching 50–60 kmph, and hailstorms at isolated places, accompanied by scattered rainfall.
The same alert and forecast extend across Punjab and Haryana, as an active western disturbance is expected to impact the region from April 3, intensifying weather activity over the next two days.
After this spell, conditions are likely to ease briefly, but another round of unstable weather under a yellow alert is forecast for Tuesday and Wednesday, with thunderstorms, lightning, and gusty winds of 30–40 kmph at isolated places, along with scattered rainfall.
Tricity forecast & temperatures
The IMD has predicted partly cloudy skies with thunderstorms and light rain activity on April 3 and 4, followed by mainly partly cloudy conditions. Maximum temperatures in the Tricity are expected to hover between 30°C and 32°C, while minimum temperatures will range between 15°C and 19°C over the next five days.
Past 24 hours weather
Chandigarh recorded a maximum temperature of 32.8°C and a minimum of 17.6°C, both near normal levels. No rainfall was recorded in the past 24 hours, while winds largely remained calm.
Across the region, weather remained largely dry in Punjab, while Haryana witnessed very light rain at isolated places. Temperatures have risen and remain above normal, with Patiala recording 36.8°C in Punjab and Gurugram touching 37.8°C in Haryana as the highest maximums.
Farmers on edge
The orange alert has triggered concern among farmers, especially as the wheat crop is at an advanced stage of harvesting across Punjab and Haryana. Strong winds and hailstorms pose a serious risk of lodging (flattening) of standing crops, while rainfall could damage already harvested produce lying in the open.
The IMD has advised farmers to suspend field operations, avoid irrigation, and protect harvested crops, warning that hail and strong winds could cause significant damage to wheat and other rabi crops.
Authorities have also cautioned residents to stay indoors during thunderstorms, avoid taking shelter under trees and keep away from electric poles and water bodies due to lightning risk.
Overall, the region is bracing for two back-to-back weather systems, with the immediate focus on the high-impact orange alert over the weekend.
